danger_signals.visual_form: contrasting colored bullets/energy bolts moving toward player
rapid appearance of adversarial units on screen edges or from terrain
danger_signals.meaning: escalating danger intensity in current sector

danger_signals.visual_form: terrain obstacles, destructible elements, or industrial/military structures
large scale entity occupying significant screen real estate with distinct silhouette
danger_signals.meaning: major progression checkpoint with heightened threat level

safety_signals.visual_form: absence of active enemies, reduced enemy spawn rate
floating collectible items with distinct visual markers
friendly unit positioning or visual distinction from hostile forces
safety_signals.meaning: faction-aligned support or safe zone indicator

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: enemy units display distinct coloration/design from player avatar and allied forces
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: military bases, fortified positions align with player faction narrative

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: contrasting sprite design, alternate uniform/armor coloration compared to player units
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: enemy installations, occupied territory, fortified enemy positions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: increasing density of enemies on screen, more aggressive projectile patterns
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy units adopt more complex attack formations, faster movement speed

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: environmental architecture shifts to reflect faction occupation (military structures, defensive placements)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies patrol or defend specific zones rather than pursuing randomly

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: background and foreground environmental assets change to reflect new geographic location or facility
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy types and attack patterns adapt to stage-specific context

environmental_storytelling.example: military/industrial environments convey occupied territory and strategic importance through defensive structures
clustered enemy positions at chokepoints or strategic locations suggest tactical preparation
destroyed structures, smoke effects, and damaged terrain indicate ongoing conflict and war damage
stage backgrounds transition through environmental color schemes to signal location changes and narrative progression

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: predictable movement patterns along defined paths or screen edges
increases firing rate, pursues player position, adjusts trajectory toward player avatar
complex multi-phase attack patterns, telegraphed special attacks, arena-dominating movement
visual feedback through sprite flashing or brief invulnerability state

notes: Gunforce II (1995, SNES) is a run-and-gun shmup employing standard visual language for threat communication. The paradigm relies on sprite density, projectile visibility, and enemy silhouette recognition. World-state is communicated through environmental progression (stage themes reflecting military/industrial settings) and enemy composition shifts. Faction identity is implicit through player vs. enemy visual contrast rather than explicit heraldic markers. The game uses standard shmup visual grammar: projectile contrast, enemy spawn telegraphing, and arena spatial design to manage player information load.
